message.content: Adversaries may use Data Obfuscation: Protocol Impersonation to disguise command and control (C2) activity by mimicking legitimate protocols or web services, blending malicious traffic with normal network traffic. This technique can interfere with security tools and evade detection. Common methods include fake SSL/TLS handshakes, mimicking HTTP protocols, and using custom SSL libraries. Notable examples include BADCALL, Bankshot, Cobalt Strike, FakeM, FALLCHILL, HARDRAIN, Higaisa, InvisiMole, KeyBoy, Lazarus Group, Okrum, SUNBURST, and TAINTEDSCRIBE. These methods can hide data in HTTP headers, URI parameters, or transaction bodies, and use modified SSL code to thwart decryption. Mitigation strategies include implementing robust network monitoring, using anomaly detection systems, and employing deep packet inspection to identify and block suspicious traffic patterns. Regular updates to security tools and protocols can also help in detecting and mitigating such obfuscation techniques.
